ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Medu Vada. Closest parent is a fried lentil product. A dedicated concept covering urad dal ring fritters is recommended.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of medu vada is 2 pieces (approximately 55–60g each, 120g total), each shaped as a ring doughnut with a crispy golden exterior and soft interior, served alongside sambar and coconut chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Medu Vada — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EI000041
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in ICMR-NIN under entry EI000041 — Medu Vada, deep-fried urad dal fritter with standard seasoning.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — urad dal-based product, estimated from whole urad dal GI; deep-frying and whole lentil structure significantly lower GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation raises fat content significantly; 2-piece serving (120g) provides approximately 21g fat and 342mg sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit medu vada to 1–2 pieces and avoid pairing with high-sodium sambar. The deep-frying oil quality also matters for cardiovascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~42) due to the protein and fibre content of urad dal and structural resistance of deep-fried batter; glycaemic load per serving is moderate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite deep-frying, medu vada has a low glycaemic index and can be consumed occasionally by diabetic patients. Limit to 1–2 pieces and monitor the sambar and chutney accompaniments for added carbohydrates.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried in vegetable oil which, if refined and reused, contributes to pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acids; urad dal itself is an excellent anti-inflammatory legume.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Urad dal is rich in protein and folate which support immune regulation. Consuming occasionally is acceptable; avoid if fried in highly re-used oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Medu Vada is one of South India's most iconic snacks — a savoury, crispy, ring-shaped fritter made from ground urad dal (dehusked black lentils) batter seasoned with cumin, black pepper, green chilli, and curry leaves. The doughnut-shaped vada is deep-fried until the exterior is golden and crunchy while the interior remains soft. High in plant protein and iron, but calorie-dense due to deep frying. In Singapore, medu vada is available at South Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and tiffin counters, always served as a pair with sambar and coconut chutney.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in ICMR-NIN under entry EI000041 — Medu Vada, standard deep-fried preparation.
notes: Vegan and gluten-free. Cholesterol is 0.0 as urad dal contains no cholesterol. Sambar and coconut chutney are not included in per_100g values — these are for the vada alone. Calorie density is driven by the deep-frying oil absorbed (~17.5g fat per 100g).
